items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Guatemala: América Latina y el Caribe: El Niño 2026-2027, junio 2026
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Countries: Guatemala, Belize, Bolivia (Plurinational State of), Colombia, Cuba, Ecuador, El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, Nicaragua, Peru, Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

El riesgo humanitario asociado al fenómeno de El Niño 2026-2027 es alto en América Latina y el Caribe (LAC). Los pronósticos indican una probabilidad de entre el 82% y el 88% de que el fenómeno se establezca entre mayo y julio de 2026, aumentando a más del 90% hacia finales de año.

La región enfrenta dos escenarios climáticos opuestos. Por un lado, el Corredor Seco Centroamericano, el Caribe y algunas zonas del norte de Sudamérica podrían experimentar condiciones más secas y calurosas, con pérdidas de cultivos superiores al 30%, deterioro de la seguridad alimentaria y una presión adicional sobre los recursos hídricos. Por otro, la costa del Pacífico sudamericano y parte de la región andina enfrentarían lluvias por encima de lo normal, con riesgos elevados de inundaciones y deslizamientos de tierra.

Este evento ocurre en un contexto particularmente desafiante, marcado por temperaturas globales récord, el incremento de los costos de producción agrícola, las disrupciones en las cadenas de suministro globales, la volatilidad de los precios de los combustibles derivada de las actuales tensiones geopolíticas y la persistencia de vulnerabilidades socioeconómicas y de seguridad en varios países.

En este contexto, se subraya la necesidad de reforzar el monitoreo, acelerar la preparación, promover acciones anticipatorias y movilizar financiamiento flexible para reducir los impactos humanitarios antes de que estos se materialicen.

items.creator: Health Cluster
items.title: Venezuela Health Cluster: Earthquakes Situation Report #3 (01 July 2026) [EN/ES]
items.author: Health Cluster
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Sources: Health Cluster, Pan American Health Organization, World Health Organization

Please refer to the attached file.

Los socios del Clúster de Salud mantienen una respuesta activa en los estados más afectados, con presencia operativa en establecimientos de salud y la movilización de 733 profesionales de salud, más de 1,17 millones de unidades de medicamentos, 4.277 kits de trauma y 47 vehículos. Asimismo, continúan los esfuerzos para el despliegue y activación de Equipos Médicos de Emergencia (EMT) internacionales que refuercen la capacidad de atención sanitaria en las zonas afectadas.

Las evaluaciones realizadas en 36 establecimientos de salud evidencian una situación crítica caracterizada por daños estructurales, sobrecarga de los servicios asistenciales, interrupciones de agua, electricidad y gas, así como importantes brechas de personal sanitario. Los hospitales reportan alta demanda de atención por trauma, acumulación de cirugías especializadas y presión sostenida sobre los servicios de emergencia y atención hospitalaria.

Persisten necesidades prioritarias de suministros médicos especializados, material de osteosíntesis, equipos para cuidados intensivos, reactivos de laboratorio, agua potable y apoyo logístico para la continuidad de los servicios de salud. Asimismo, se registra una alta demanda de servicios de salud mental y apoyo psicosocial, así como la necesidad de fortalecer mecanismos de protección para grupos vulnerables.

Los socios continúan apoyando la evaluación de daños, la prestación de servicios esenciales de salud, la gestión de suministros humanitarios y la ampliación de la cobertura operativa hacia otros estados afectados. Adicionalmente, se mantiene la vigilancia ante el riesgo de brotes asociados a las afectaciones en agua y saneamiento, las condiciones de alojamiento temporal y las vulnerabilidades epidemiológicas preexistentes.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Venezuela (República Bolivariana de): Respuesta a Terremotos - Reporte de Situación #4 (1 de julio de 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

El cuarto Informe de Situación presenta una actualización sobre el impacto de los terremotos del 24 de junio en la República Bolivariana de Venezuela y resume la respuesta en curso y planificada de la OIM en apoyo a las poblaciones afectadas y a los esfuerzos nacionales.

Aspectos destacados

Las cifras oficiales reportan al menos 2.295 personas fallecidas, 11.267 heridas, 12.841 personas severamente afectadas y 26.403 personas afectadas tras los terremotos del 24 de junio. Las cifras pendientes de nueva actualización incluyen 855 edificaciones afectadas, 189 colapsos totales y 6.461 personas rescatadas.

Los daños a viviendas e infraestructura urbana continúan generando desplazamiento y necesidades de alojamiento temporal, incluidas familias en campamentos transitorios y personas que duermen fuera de viviendas dañadas por preocupaciones de seguridad o para proteger sus pertenencias.

La OIM continúa apoyando actividades de alojamiento temporal, WASH, salud y SMAPS, protección y gestión de información, incluido el apoyo a sitios temporales en La Guaira y la provisión de 809 servicios de asistencia en salud a personas afectadas.

Actividades de la OIM

La OIM ha continuado apoyando alojamiento, coordinación de sitios y servicios esenciales en campamentos transitorios. En el Polideportivo José María Vargas, se organizaron literas con apoyo de líderes comunitarios, se reorganizaron insumos para distribuciones y se reforzaron los procesos de registro e ingreso/salida. La OIM también instaló la primera de siete carpas previstas para proporcionar espacio de descanso para 100 personas. En el Estadio César Nieves, la OIM inició la instalación de espacios con sombra para apoyar la prestación de servicios y a las poblaciones afectadas.

En salud, SMAPS, WASH, protección y datos, la OIM y sus socios han proporcionado 809 servicios de asistencia en salud mediante apoyo directo y brigadas médicas. El 1 de julio, la asistencia incluyó pediatría, ginecología y obstetricia, medicina general e interna, consultas individuales de SMAPS, primeros auxilios psicológicos y cuatro referencias médicas. La OIM también apoyó la llegada de una planta portátil de tratamiento de agua a Maiquetía, continuó brindando orientación en protección y apoyo a referencias de casos, actualizó el mapeo de alojamientos temporales, confirmó el uso de la herramienta IMS de registro de beneficiarios y participó con OCHA en el proceso de Evaluación Rápida de Necesidades.

Respuesta planificada de la OIM

La respuesta planificada de la OIM continuará priorizando alojamiento temporal seguro, coordinación y gestión de sitios, WASH, salud y SMAPS, protección, datos y evaluaciones de necesidades.

En los campamentos transitorios, la OIM continuará apoyando la planificación de sitios, el registro, la gestión de almacenes y la instalación de carpas y arreglos básicos para mejorar la seguridad, la dignidad y la protección. Esto se complementará con apoyo a la organización comunitaria, normas de convivencia, provisión de información y mecanismos de rendición de cuentas, incluidos AAP, mitigación de riesgos de VBG y medidas de PEAS.

En WASH y salud, la OIM se enfocará en ampliar el acceso a agua segura, promoción de higiene, saneamiento básico y servicios móviles de salud en comunidades afectadas y sitios temporales. El apoyo en salud priorizará equipos médicos, referencias, atención de enfermedades crónicas, salud sexual y reproductiva, primeros auxilios psicológicos y SMAPS, junto con la provisión de medicamentos esenciales, insumos y dispositivos de asistencia para personas con necesidades específicas.

En protección y gestión de información, la OIM continuará el monitoreo de riesgos, las referencias seguras y el apoyo a personas con necesidades específicas, incluidos niños, niñas y adolescentes, personas mayores, mujeres embarazadas y lactantes, y personas afectadas por la pérdida o daño de documentos de identidad. La OIM también apoyará Evaluaciones Rápidas de Necesidades, mientras produce perfiles de sitios, actualizaciones de movilidad, mapas y análisis de necesidades para orientar la priorización multisectorial.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of): Earthquake Response Situation Report #4 (1 July 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

This fourth Situation Report provides an updated overview of the impact of the 24 June earthquakes in the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela and outlines IOM’s ongoing and planned response in support of affected populations and national efforts.

Highlights

Official figures report at least 2,295 fatalities, 11,267 injuries, 12,841 people severely affected and 26,403 people affected following the 24 June earthquakes. Figures pending further update include 855 buildings affected, 189 total collapses and 6,461 people rescued.

Damage to housing and urban infrastructure continues to drive displacement and temporary accommodation needs, including among families in temporary sites and people sleeping outside damaged homes due to safety concerns or to protect their belongings.

IOM continues supporting temporary accommodation, WASH, health and MHPSS, protection and information management activities, including support to temporary sites in La Guaira and the provision of 809 health assistance services to affected people.

IOM activities

IOM has continued supporting shelter, site coordination and essential services in temporary accommodation sites. At Polideportivo José María Vargas, bunk beds were arranged with support from community leaders, supplies were reorganized for distributions, and registration and entry/exit processes were reinforced. IOM also installed the first of seven tents planned to provide sleeping space for 100 people. At Estadio César Nieves, IOM began installing shaded spaces to support service provision and affected populations.

In health, MHPSS, WASH, protection and data, IOM and partners have provided 809 health assistance services through direct support and medical brigades. On 1 July, assistance included paediatrics, gynaecology and obstetrics, general and internal medicine, individual MHPSS consultations, psychological first aid and four medical referrals. IOM also supported the arrival of one portable water treatment plant in Maiquetía, continued protection orientation and referral support, updated temporary accommodation mapping, confirmed the use of an IMS registration tool and participated with OCHA in the Rapid Needs Assessment process.

IOM planned response

IOM’s planned response will continue prioritizing safe temporary accommodation, site coordination and management, WASH, health and MHPSS, protection, and data and needs assessments.

In temporary accommodation sites, IOM will continue supporting site planning, registration, warehouse management and the installation of tents and basic site arrangements to improve safety, dignity and protection. This will be complemented by support to community organization, coexistence rules, information provision and accountability mechanisms, including AAP, GBV risk mitigation and PSEA measures.

In WASH and health, IOM will focus on expanding access to safe water, hygiene promotion, basic sanitation and mobile health services in affected communities and temporary sites. Health support will prioritize medical teams, referrals, chronic disease care, sexual and reproductive health, psychological first aid and MHPSS, alongside the provision of essential medicines, supplies and assistive devices for people with specific needs.

In protection and information management, IOM will continue risk monitoring, safe referrals and support to people with specific needs, including children and adolescents, older persons, pregnant and lactating women, and people affected by the loss or damage of identity documents. IOM will also support Rapid Needs Assessments, while producing site profiles, mobility updates, maps and needs analyses to inform multisectoral prioritization.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Terremotos en Venezuela junio de 2026: Equipos de Búsqueda y Rescate Urbano (USAR) - Datos clave (Al 2 de julio, 10:00 GMT-4)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

A solicitud del Gobierno de Venezuela, equipos USAR de 28 países, con más de 2.200 integrantes y 175 perros de búsqueda, han llegado al país para localizar y rescatar a personas atrapadas bajo estructuras colapsadas y brindar atención médica inicial. La Oficina de Coordinación de Asuntos Humanitarios de las Naciones Unidas (OCHA) ha activado sus herramientas de respuesta a emergencias y está apoyando a los equipos.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Venezuela Earthquakes June 2026: Urban Search and Rescue (USAR) Team Snapshot (As of 2 July, 10:00 GMT-4)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

At the request of the Government of Venezuela, USAR teams from 28 countries, with more than 2,200 personnel and 175 search dogs, have arrived in the country to locate and extract people trapped by collapsed structures and provide initial medical support. The United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) has activated its emergency response tools and is supporting the teams.

items.creator: International Medical Corps
items.title: Middle East Conflict: Lebanon Response Situation Report #8 | July 2, 2026
items.author: International Medical Corps
items.content:
Country: Lebanon
Source: International Medical Corps

Please refer to the attached file.

FAST FACTS

  • The number of registered displaced people In Lebanon has fallen to 704,445 and occupancy in the shelters has decreased by more than 50%.

OUR FOOTPRINT

  • International Medical Corps maintains a wide operational presence across the Middle East, delivering lifesaving assistance in active conflict zones and high-risk environments.

OUR RESPONSE

  • Across the region, International Medical Corps sustains integrated health, nutrition, mental health, protection, and water, sanitation and hygiene programming through mobile teams, field hospitals and fixed facilities, with established systems in place to expand rapidly as needs intensify.
  • In Lebanon, 37 mobile medical teams have operated across 143 shelters and community sites, delivering 22,524 consultations, treating 11,809 acute cases and managing 2,516 chronic conditions. Teams have also conducted health awareness sessions for 3,389 people, referred 1,324 patients to specialized care and vaccinated 271 children.
  • Nutrition teams have screened 1,138 children and pregnant or breastfeeding women for malnutrition, while mental health teams have reached 1,612 people with psychosocial and psychological support services. In addition, teams have distributed 2,485 hygiene kits to displaced and vulnerable households and individuals.

International Medical Corps is continuing to deliver lifesaving health services at scale across the Middle East, supporting communities affected by conflict, displacement and worsening humanitarian conditions. Our regional presence and operational reach are set out in previous Situation Reports and in our regional Capability Statement. All personnel remain safe and accounted for.

Across the Middle East region, humanitarian needs remain critical and deeply complex. International Medical Corps continues to adapt our operations across the region, drawing on decades of experience, trusted local partnerships and well-established emergency response systems. This enables us to deliver timely, effective and lifesaving assistance to communities affected by crisis, while remaining responsive to evolving humanitarian needs.

Country: Lebanon
Source: International Medical Corps

Teams have also conducted health awareness sessions for 3,389 people, referred 1,324 patients to specialized care and vaccinated 271 children.
Nutrition teams have screened 1,138 children and pregnant or breastfeeding women for malnutrition, while mental health teams have reached 1,612 people with psychosocial and psychological support services. In addition, teams have distributed 2,485 hygiene kits to displaced and vulnerable households and individuals.
International Medical Corps is continuing to deliver lifesaving health services at scale across the Middle East, supporting communities affected by conflict, displacement and worsening humanitarian conditions. Our regional presence and operational reach are set out in previous Situation Reports and in our regional Capability Statement. All personnel remain safe and accounted for.
Across the Middle East region, humanitarian needs remain critical and deeply complex. International Medical Corps continues to adapt our operations across the region, drawing on decades of experience, trusted local partnerships and well-established emergency response systems. This enables us to deliver timely, effective and lifesaving assistance to communities affected by crisis, while remaining responsive to evolving humanitarian needs.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Ukraine — Social Cohesion in Ukraine: Trust, Belonging and Civic Participation — July 2026
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Ukraine
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

This analytical brief examines social cohesion in Ukraine, drawing on data from Round 23 of IOM’s General Population Survey, collected between January and March 2026.

The brief analyses several dimensions of social cohesion, including trust between individuals and institutions, sense of belonging, civic participation, and relations between different population groups, including internally displaced persons, returnees, veterans, and non-displaced populations. The analysis considers how these dimensions interact in a context marked by ongoing displacement, economic strain, and pressure on local governance and service delivery.

The brief examines where social cohesion remains strong, where it is weakening, and the implications for transition and recovery programming. The findings are intended to inform evidence-based programming to support more targeted and integrated approaches to strengthening social cohesion in Ukraine.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Niger — Profil des voyageurs au nord du Niger (janvier - mars 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Niger
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

Entre janvier et mars 2026, le nord du Niger s’est imposé comme un carrefour stratégique des migrations régionales et internationales. Au total, 302 892 voyageurs y ont été enregistrés aux points de suivi de l’Organisation Internationale pour les Migrations (OIM), dans le prolongement d’une forte mobilité déjà observée en 2025 (plus de 1,2 million de passages au Nord).

Les flux sont dominés par les mouvements internes (43%) et les flux sortants (40%), contre 17 pour cent d’entrées, confirmant le rôle du Niger à la fois comme espace de circulation et de départ. Le profil des voyageurs est plutôt masculin (84%) et composé d’une majorité d’adultes, traduisant des migrations principalement économiques.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Pakistan — Flow Monitoring of Afghan Returnees — Bi-Weekly Report (1 – 15 June 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Countries: Pakistan, Afghanistan
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

The International Organization for Migration (IOM) Pakistan’s FM provides key insights into cross-border migration trends, returning Afghan nationals to Afghanistan, their profiles, mobility patterns, routes, vulnerabilities, demographics and other social characteristics. Between 1 and 15 June 2026, DTM border monitors (BMs) interviewed 1,764 heads of households through a Flow Monitoring Registry (FMR) form, while a total of 58,469 Afghan returnees were identified through the five border crossing points (BCPs).

Highlights

  • Cumulatively, from 15 September 2023 to 15 June 2026, 2,507,000 individuals have returned.
  • During the reporting period, the number of returns increased by 5 per cent and deportations by 49 per cent compared to the previous reporting period (16-31 May 2026). The increase in returns and deportations reflects the resumption of cross-border movements and arrest operations following the Eid period.
  • Since 1 April 2025, 1,652,967 Afghan nationals returned, ofwhich 211,674 (13%) were deported.
  • Fear of arrest (96%) remained the most important reasonamong respondents to return to Afghanistan.

Highlights
Cumulatively, from 15 September 2023 to 15 June 2026, 2,507,000 individuals have returned.
During the reporting period, the number of returns increased by 5 per cent and deportations by 49 per cent compared to the previous reporting period (16-31 May 2026). The increase in returns and deportations reflects the resumption of cross-border movements and arrest operations following the Eid period.
Since 1 April 2025, 1,652,967 Afghan nationals returned, ofwhich 211,674 (13%) were deported.
Fear of arrest (96%) remained the most important reasonamong respondents to return to Afghanistan.

items.creator: European Union Agency for Asylum
items.title: World: EUAA publishes guides on accelerated examination and qualification for international protection, as new European rules begin to apply
items.author: European Union Agency for Asylum
items.content:
Country: World
Source: European Union Agency for Asylum

With the Pact on Migration and Asylum now applicable across Europe, the EUAA has published a Practical Guide outlining the grounds on which EU countries must now apply the new accelerated examination procedure to asylum claims. In parallel, the Agency has also published an updated Practical Guide on Qualification which helps Member States decide who qualifies for refugee status and subsidiary protection status under the new rules.

The European Union Agency for Asylum has just published a Practical Guide on a key element of the Asylum Procedure Regulation, the mandatory accelerated examination procedure, which became applicable on 12 June 2026. It will support Member States in quickly identifying people that may not need international protection in the EU, and whose claims must now be assessed within 3 months. Importantly, while the timeframe to appeal a decision made in the accelerated procedure is shorter, all basic principles, safeguards and guarantees apply, and applicants may challenge the decision of the asylum procedure in line with their right to judicial remedy.

The new practical guide is mainly aimed at asylum case officers, interviewers and decision-makers in Member States and outlines the 10 different grounds on which national authorities should apply the accelerated examination of claims, and the underlying considerations that apply. The grounds for acceleration can include, for example, that:

  • The claim is clearly unconvincing or that an applicant has intentionally provided false information or disposed of their identity documents. In these circumstances, national authorities must have objective indications that either is the case; including, for example, of the applicant having destroyed an identity document to prevent Member States from establishing their nationality.
  • Applicants from a Safe Country of Origin or from a country for which the EU average recognition rate stood at or below 20% may have the examination of their claims accelerated. In 2025, over one quarter of asylum applications (26 %) lodged in EU+ countries were from nationals on the EU-level list of Safe Countries of Origin, and around half by nationals from countries under the 20% threshold.
  • Unlawful entry – without lodging an application as soon as possible – or residing lawfully in the EU and then applying to extend a legal stay or, vice versa to delay, frustrate or prevent a removal may all be grounds for accelerating the examination of claims. Finally, applicants raising issues not relevant to international protection, for example, socio-economic reasons may see the handling of their application accelerated.

Despite swifter timelines under the new European rules, accelerated examination of a claim does not per se mean that it is unfounded. To support case officers in their assessments of who qualifies for international protection, the EUAA has published an updated Practical Guide on Qualification for International Protection. Aimed at case officers, interviewers and decision-makers, the guide updates the previous edition (2018) and supports EU+ countries in determining who qualifies for refugee status or subsidiary protection status.

The practical guide includes ‘decision trees’ that guide case officers when examining whether an applicant qualifies for international protection. This should be read together with the country-specific common analysis and guidance the Agency makes available on a series of countries of origin. A dedicated poster and checklist for ascertaining who qualifies for international protection will be made available soon.

The Agency’s practical guides and tools help increase the knowledge and technical skills of asylum practitioners across the EU. While not legally binding on Member States, asylum practitioners consider them to be soft law instruments, which are primarily for use by EU+ national authorities but are also often referred to by civil society organisations and by European courts and national judiciary bodies. Therefore, they are commonly developed by – and agreed with – members of the EUAA’s thematic networks and are subsequently adopted by the EUAA Management Board.

items.creator: Insecurity Insight
items.title: World: Aid in Danger Bi-Monthly News Brief (10 - 23 June 2026)
items.author: Insecurity Insight
items.content:
Countries: World, Afghanistan, Central African Republic, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Haiti, India, Lebanon, occupied Palestinian territory, Sudan
Source: Insecurity Insight

Please refer to the attached file.

Protection of Humanitarian and UN Personnel, Premises and Assets
In the 25 months since the adoption of the resolution, Insecurity Insight recorded 1,258 attacks and acts of violence against aid workers from the UN, humanitarian and civil society, resulting in 787 aid workers killed, 717 injured, 371 kidnapped and 576 arrested. These acts of violence underscore the urgent need for strengthened collective action and the promotion of collaborative security risk mitigation strategies to enhance aid worker safety and security and to provide crisis and post-incident support to affected individuals and their families.

Of the 787 aid workers who were killed, 610 were national aid workers, representing 78% of total killings, while ten were international aid workers. These figures underscore how aid workers closest to affected communities - often working with the least protection and visibility - bear the greatest risk.

Download the data (updated every Monday). Insecurity Insight continuously backdates data, as such, numbers may change if or when further information is made available.

items.creator: Insecurity Insight
items.title: DR Congo: Anticipatory Action and Risk Mitigation of Food Insecurity in Eastern DRC - January 2023 to May 2026
items.author: Insecurity Insight
items.content:
Country: Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: Insecurity Insight

Please refer to the attached file.

Insecurity Insight has released a new report on anticipatory action and risk mitigation for food insecurity in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, analysing how conflict-related violence continues to damage local food systems and undermine livelihoods long after individual incidents occur. The report covers the period from January 2023 to May 2026 and explores how humanitarian actors can move from reactive responses towards proactive risk mitigation.

Our latest report analyses:

  • 465 incidents of food-related violence recorded across eastern DRC between January 2023 and May 2026
  • Violence affecting food production, transport, markets, livestock and humanitarian food assistance across conflict-affected communities
  • Geographic patterns of violence across eastern DRC, with incidents heavily concentrated in North Kivu and Ituri provinces
  • The cumulative and long-term impacts of attacks, displacement, extortion and restrictions on agricultural access on local food systems

Key findings:

Food-related violence was overwhelmingly concentrated in two provinces. North Kivu accounted for 55% of all recorded incidents and Ituri for 37%, together they represented 92% of all documented food-related violence during the monitoring period.

People, not infrastructure, bore the brunt of attacks. Nearly two-thirds of incidents (61%) involved violence against people engaged in food-related activities, including farmers working their fields, traders and marketgoers, and humanitarian workers delivering food assistance.

Livestock theft and destruction remained a major driver of insecurity. One quarter of all incidents involved the looting, killing or injuring of livestock, directly undermining household income, food availability and community resilience.

The most damaging impacts often unfold long after the violence itself. Landmines, displacement, abandoned fields and disrupted harvest cycles mean that even where incident numbers decline, food insecurity can continue to worsen months or years later.

Extortion and informal taxation have become structural pressures on food systems. Armed actors, including FARDC, Wazalendo, M23, Mayi Mayi militias and ADF, routinely imposed illegal payments, erected checkpoints and restricted access to agricultural land, creating persistent barriers to production and trade.

The report argues that humanitarian responses must shift from reacting to food crises after they emerge to anticipating predictable consequences of conflict violence and acting earlier to protect livelihoods, sustain local food systems and reduce humanitarian need.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Central African Republic: Situation Report No. 74, as of 30 June, 2026
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Central African Republic
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

HIGHLIGHTS

  • Deaths, injuries and population displacement following clashes in Am-Dafock, Vakaga prefecture.
  • Escalating violence and access constraints continue to exacerbate the humanitarian crisis in Haut-Mbomou prefecture.
  • 200 vulnerable households receive seeds and farming tools in Koui, Ouham-Pendé prefecture.

GENERAL CONTEXT

Vakaga Prefecture – North-East

  • Civilians were killed and injured, while a significant number of people—mostly women and children—sought refuge at the MINUSCA Force base following an attack by armed elements in Am-Dafock on 29 June. Clashes continued on 30 June, further deteriorating the security situation and triggering additional displacement, the scale of which remains unknown. Humanitarian access in the area is expected to become even more constrained, as humanitarian actors suspended movements last week following an armed robbery incident. This disruption is affecting the delivery of life-saving assistance to at least 34,000 vulnerable people, particularly in the sectors of health, food security and protection. Limited mobile network coverage continues to hamper information-sharing from Am-Dafock. In Birao, the capital of Vakaga Prefecture, shops and businesses remained closed on 30 June due to insecurity-related fears. The town currently hosts around 25,000 Sudanese refugees—approximately twice its resident population—placing considerable pressure on already scarce local resources and further complicating efforts to respond to growing humanitarian needs.

HUMANITARIAN NEEDS AND RESPONSE

Multi-sector

Haut-Mbomou Prefecture – South-East

  • The NGO Technical Support for Community Development Cooperation (CATDEC), a partner of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR), provided cash assistance to 249 households in Zémio from 24 to 26 June. The beneficiaries included internally displaced persons (IDPs), returnees, and members of the host community caring for individuals with specific needs. This intervention aimed to strengthen the livelihoods of the most vulnerable individuals within these households. The activity was carried out with the support of the Central Emergency Response Fund (CERF).

Health

Ouham-Pendé Prefecture – West

  • The Rehabilitation Center for People with Physical Disabilities (CHRAM) provided medical care to approximately 500 vulnerable individuals, including internally displaced persons (IDPs), returnees, and members of the host community. Through its mobile clinic, the organization conducted activities from 22 to 26 June along the Bozoum–Bocaranga, Bozoum–Bouar, and Bozoum–Paoua axes, offering consultations on maternal and child health as well as malnutrition.In addition, 80 pregnant women received micronutrient supplements (iron and folic acid) during antenatal care consultations at five health facilities in the region. Supported by the Central African Republic Humanitarian Fund (CAR HF), these activities aim to strengthen disease prevention, improve nutrition, and promote good health practices within the community.

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: WFP South Sudan Country Brief, June 2026
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Countries: South Sudan, Sudan
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

SITUATION OVERVIEW
• The political and security situation in South
Sudan remains tense, as compounding armed
conflict, climatic shocks, and economic instability
continue to drive a severe humanitarian crisis.
• The displacement crisis remains severe.
Between January and May, violence displaced
more than 420,000 people across South Sudan,
including 324,000 in Jonglei State alone. As of 31
May, the country hosts a total of 2 million
internally displaced people (IDPs).
• The conflict in Sudan has forced 1.39 million
people into South Sudan, severely exacerbating
the humanitarian situation. As of 30 April, South Sudan hosted 628,000 refugees, including recent
arrivals.
• The Integrated Food Security Phase
Classification (IPC) update projects that 7.8
million people will face acute food insecurity (IPC
Phase 3 or worse) between April and July 2026.
Of this, 2.5 million people are expected to face
IPC Phase 4 (Emergency) and 73,000 IPC Phase 5
(Catastrophe). In addition, approximately 3.4
million pregnant and breastfeeding women, and
children aged 6–59 months, require urgent
nutrition assistance to address acute
malnutrition.
• South Sudan is grappling with an ongoing
cholera crisis. Since declaring the outbreak in
October 2024, the country has reported 103,000
cases and 1,670 fatalities.
